es.text: Rotation reminders, post-quantum preparation, and annual draft-signature checks.
{count} item(s) need review. Open Key Health for details.
xKey stores a separate encrypted one-time-signature reserve and a public commitment for future migration workflows. It does not change how current blockchains verify transactions.
Fewer reminders, suitable for rarely used wallets.
No transaction is sent to the blockchain.
Only checks that you still hold the right key.
Only wallets currently visible after filters.
Render revealed private keys and seed phrases as session-only pixels instead of normal interface text.
Revealed secrets are drawn as pixels
Revealed secrets use normal text rendering

When enabled, xKey avoids placing revealed private keys or seed phrases into normal DOM text. Sensitive values are drawn to a canvas with a randomized in-memory glyph session, reducing risk from text dumps and accessibility-style extraction. This does not protect against cameras, OCR, screenshots, or malware with full control of the device.
Vault opens only with this device hardware
Compatibility fallback is kept to reduce lockout risk

When enabled, xKey wraps the vault decryption key with Android Keystore/StrongBox when supported. That key cannot be exported from the device, so copied app data will not open by itself on another machine.

xKey still encrypts the vault with its internal AES key, but that AES key is wrapped by a non-exportable hardware-backed key. StrongBox is preferred when the device supports it; otherwise Android Keystore is used as the device-bound protection layer.
Unlock the vault before changing hardware-bound mode.
Set a device screen lock before enabling this mode.
Hardware-only mode disabled and compatibility fallback restored
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Controls Android's secure window flag for xKey. By default, screenshots and screen recording are allowed so you can document normal app screens when needed.

When enabled, Android blocks screenshots, recent-app previews, and screen recording for this app window. Turning this on or off requires the reveal password so someone holding an unlocked device cannot quietly weaken this protection.
Set a reveal password before changing screenshot protection.
Enter the reveal password to change this setting.
Enter your reveal password to block screenshots and screen recording.
Enter your reveal password to allow screenshots and screen recording.
Confirm this change in the shared reveal password box below.
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Password required before revealing sensitive values
No extra password before revealing sensitive values
This one password is used when revealing private keys or seed phrases and when changing screenshot/recording protection. Keeping both controls here avoids separate passwords that are easy to confuse.
Action history is a local convenience log and can be cleared. Security audit log entries above remain separate.

Choose “Seed Phrase” if you want each vanity wallet saved with its mnemonic for easier backup, or choose “Private Key” for faster scanning. If both cannot be provided in a given flow, prefer the seed phrase for safer recovery and broader wallet compatibility.
Vanity search runs inside this device. Private keys are created locally and are not sent to a server. Use short patterns for faster results and always back up the seed/private key after saving.
Avoid running while the device is hot from charging or under heavy load.
Place the device in an airy spot; use a cooling stand for long runs.
Scanning locally on this device. If it gets hot, pause, shorten the pattern, or switch to Eco/Balanced mode.
Vanity generation keeps the CPU busy trying many keys, so the device can heat up quickly and consume more power.
Avoid running continuously for too long on poorly cooled laptops or mobile phones.
If the device gets hot, please stop the search, shorten the pattern length, or take a few minutes break.
Do not block the cooling vents.
Not enough available storage for this operation.
Choose which secondary patterns are worth keeping during the scan. Each enabled rule can have its own minimum length.
Keeps addresses with repeated characters at the beginning or end, such as 1111 or aaaa.
Keeps forward hex sequences such as 1234, 4567, abcd.
Keeps reverse hex sequences such as 9876, dcba, 3210.
Keeps addresses where both the start and end contain attractive repeated runs.
Keeps addresses where the ending mirrors the beginning, such as abc...cba.
Keeps addresses with matching leading and trailing blocks, such as 123...123.
Keeps addresses with a palindrome-looking edge.
Keeps alternating pairs such as ababab or 121212.
Keeps common lucky strings or your comma-separated custom hex patterns.
Roll a physical dice and input the results
Derive a new mnemonic from a master seed without storing the master seed in xKey.
Master seed or extended private key
Paste a BIP39 seed phrase or xprv/zprv...
The master seed is used only in memory for this derivation and is not saved by xKey.
Enter a master seed or extended private key.
Invalid master seed or extended private key.
Could not derive the BIP85 child phrase.
This is the starting point. The computer generates a completely random sequence of bits (0s and 1s). This sequence is usually 128 bits (for 12 words) or 256 bits (for 24 words).
This is the security 'core' of the wallet. If someone guesses this entropy, they control your wallet.
The entropy is hashed (usually using SHA-256). The first few bits of the hash are taken as a Checksum and appended to the entropy.
Ensures the final seed phrase is valid and prevents typos when entering it.
The combined sequence (Entropy + Checksum) is divided into 11-bit segments. Each segment maps to an index (0-2047) in the BIP-39 wordlist.
This creates your 12 or 24-word Seed Phrase.
The Mnemonic words are run through a key stretching function (PBKDF2) using HMAC-SHA512 with 2048 iterations.
A 512-bit Master Seed is created. This seed can restore all your accounts.
The Master Seed is used to derive a specific Private Key using an HD Derivation Path (e.g., m/44'/60'/0'/0/0).
Your actual 256-bit Private Key for this specific wallet.
The Private Key is passed through the Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (secp256k1) to calculate a coordinate on the curve.
The Public Key is hashed using Keccak-256. The last 20 bytes (40 hex characters) are kept, and '0x' is added to the beginning.

xKey stores a separate encrypted one-time-signature reserve and a public commitment for future migration workflows. It does not change how current blockchains verify transactions.

Only checks that you still hold the right key.
Only wallets currently visible after filters.
No portable backup has been exported on this device yet.
Your vault has changed since the latest verified backup. Create a new backup before continuing sensitive changes.
Render revealed private keys and seed phrases as session-only pixels instead of normal interface text.
Revealed secrets are drawn as pixels
Revealed secrets use normal text rendering

When enabled, xKey avoids placing revealed private keys or seed phrases into normal DOM text. Sensitive values are drawn to a canvas with a randomized in-memory glyph session, reducing risk from text dumps and accessibility-style extraction. This does not protect against cameras, OCR, screenshots, or malware with full control of the device.
Block vault access on rooted, debug, or ADB-enabled devices
Device risk checks are recorded but not enforced
When enabled, xKey checks the Android runtime before opening the vault. If root traces, su, test-keys, a debuggable build, or ADB are detected, xKey blocks vault access and writes an audit event.
Vault opens only with this device hardware
Compatibility fallback is kept to reduce lockout risk

When enabled, xKey wraps the vault decryption key with Android Keystore/StrongBox when supported. That key cannot be exported from the device, so copied app data will not open by itself on another machine.

xKey still encrypts the vault with its internal AES key, but that AES key is wrapped by a non-exportable hardware-backed key. StrongBox is preferred when the device supports it; otherwise Android Keystore is used as the device-bound protection layer.
Unlock the vault before changing hardware-bound mode.
Set a device screen lock before enabling this mode.
Hardware-only mode disabled and compatibility fallback restored
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Controls Android's secure window flag for xKey. By default, screenshots and screen recording are allowed so you can document normal app screens when needed.

When enabled, Android blocks screenshots, recent-app previews, and screen recording for this app window. Turning this on or off requires the reveal password so someone holding an unlocked device cannot quietly weaken this protection.
Set a reveal password before changing screenshot protection.
Enter the reveal password to change this setting.
Enter your reveal password to block screenshots and screen recording.
Enter your reveal password to allow screenshots and screen recording.
Confirm this change in the shared reveal password box below.
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Password required before revealing sensitive values
No extra password before revealing sensitive values
This one password is used when revealing private keys or seed phrases and when changing screenshot/recording protection. Keeping both controls here avoids separate passwords that are easy to confuse.
Action history is a local convenience log and can be cleared. Security audit log entries above remain separate.

Choose “Seed Phrase” if you want each vanity wallet saved with its mnemonic for easier backup, or choose “Private Key” for faster scanning. If both cannot be provided in a given flow, prefer the seed phrase for safer recovery and broader wallet compatibility.
Vanity search runs inside this device. Private keys are created locally and are not sent to a server. Use short patterns for faster results and always back up the seed/private key after saving.
Avoid running while the device is hot from charging or under heavy load.
Place the device in an airy spot; use a cooling stand for long runs.
Scanning locally on this device. If it gets hot, pause, shorten the pattern, or switch to Eco/Balanced mode.
Vanity generation keeps the CPU busy trying many keys, so the device can heat up quickly and consume more power.
Avoid running continuously for too long on poorly cooled laptops or mobile phones.
If the device gets hot, please stop the search, shorten the pattern length, or take a few minutes break.
Do not block the cooling vents.
Not enough available storage for this operation.
Choose which secondary patterns are worth keeping during the scan. Each enabled rule can have its own minimum length.
Keeps addresses with repeated characters at the beginning or end, such as 1111 or aaaa.
Keeps forward hex sequences such as 1234, 4567, abcd.
Keeps reverse hex sequences such as 9876, dcba, 3210.
Keeps addresses where both the start and end contain attractive repeated runs.
Keeps addresses where the ending mirrors the beginning, such as abc...cba.
Keeps addresses with matching leading and trailing blocks, such as 123...123.
Keeps addresses with a palindrome-looking edge.
Keeps alternating pairs such as ababab or 121212.
Keeps common lucky strings or your comma-separated custom hex patterns.
Roll a physical dice and input the results
Derive a new mnemonic from a master seed without storing the master seed in xKey.
Master seed or extended private key
Paste a BIP39 seed phrase or xprv/zprv...
The master seed is used only in memory for this derivation and is not saved by xKey.
Enter a master seed or extended private key.
Invalid master seed or extended private key.
Could not derive the BIP85 child phrase.

pt.text: Rotation reminders, post-quantum preparation, and annual draft-signature checks.
{count} item(s) need review. Open Key Health for details.
xKey stores a separate encrypted one-time-signature reserve and a public commitment for future migration workflows. It does not change how current blockchains verify transactions.
Fewer reminders, suitable for rarely used wallets.
No transaction is sent to the blockchain.
Only checks that you still hold the right key.
Only wallets currently visible after filters.
Render revealed private keys and seed phrases as session-only pixels instead of normal interface text.
Revealed secrets are drawn as pixels
Revealed secrets use normal text rendering

When enabled, xKey avoids placing revealed private keys or seed phrases into normal DOM text. Sensitive values are drawn to a canvas with a randomized in-memory glyph session, reducing risk from text dumps and accessibility-style extraction. This does not protect against cameras, OCR, screenshots, or malware with full control of the device.
Vault opens only with this device hardware
Compatibility fallback is kept to reduce lockout risk

When enabled, xKey wraps the vault decryption key with Android Keystore/StrongBox when supported. That key cannot be exported from the device, so copied app data will not open by itself on another machine.

xKey still encrypts the vault with its internal AES key, but that AES key is wrapped by a non-exportable hardware-backed key. StrongBox is preferred when the device supports it; otherwise Android Keystore is used as the device-bound protection layer.
Unlock the vault before changing hardware-bound mode.
Set a device screen lock before enabling this mode.
Hardware-only mode disabled and compatibility fallback restored
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Controls Android's secure window flag for xKey. By default, screenshots and screen recording are allowed so you can document normal app screens when needed.

When enabled, Android blocks screenshots, recent-app previews, and screen recording for this app window. Turning this on or off requires the reveal password so someone holding an unlocked device cannot quietly weaken this protection.
Set a reveal password before changing screenshot protection.
Enter the reveal password to change this setting.
Enter your reveal password to block screenshots and screen recording.
Enter your reveal password to allow screenshots and screen recording.
Confirm this change in the shared reveal password box below.
Screenshots and screen recording are blocked
Screenshots and screen recording are allowed
Password required before revealing sensitive values
No extra password before revealing sensitive values
